---
title: "Coursework"
toc: true
toc-location: right
---

## Learning Goals

<div class="goal-card">

I want this page to do more than list course names.
My goal is to show how I am learning to move from **business intuition** to **empirical reasoning**.

Through graduate study, I want to become better at:

- framing messy business questions as researchable analytical problems
- distinguishing correlation from causal interpretation
- explaining quantitative evidence in plain language
- connecting statistical tools to real decision settings rather than treating them as isolated techniques

</div>

## Selected Writing from Coursework

<div class="course-card">
  <h3><a href="AB_Testing.html">A/B Testing (MGTA 495)</a></h3>
  <div class="course-meta">Experiment design, uncertainty, and decision-making under evidence</div>

  <p>
    This post starts with a simple newsletter sign-up experiment, but the real focus is not the coding.
    It is the logic of experimentation: what random assignment buys us, why sampling uncertainty matters,
    and how treatment effects can be interpreted through both hypothesis testing and regression.
  </p>

  <div class="topic-label">Key ideas</div>
  <ul>
    <li>experimental design</li>
    <li>bootstrap standard errors</li>
    <li>central limit theorem</li>
    <li>large-sample inference</li>
    <li>regression as an interpretation tool</li>
  </ul>
</div>

<div class="course-card">
  <h3><a href="Causal_Identification.html">Causal Identification (MGTA 495)</a></h3>
  <div class="course-meta">A classic policy paper, rewritten as a readable causal story</div>

  <p>
    This post revisits Card and Krueger’s study of New Jersey’s 1992 minimum wage increase.
    Instead of reproducing the paper section by section, I focus on the identification logic:
    why eastern Pennsylvania works as a comparison group, what difference-in-differences removes,
    and why the paper’s stronger tests matter for credibility.
  </p>

  <div class="topic-label">Key ideas</div>
  <ul>
    <li>difference-in-differences</li>
    <li>treatment and control group logic</li>
    <li>treatment intensity</li>
    <li>specification checks</li>
    <li>causal interpretation in observational data</li>
  </ul>
</div>
